document: Guidelines on Outsourcing Financial Institutions Other than Banks updated-1 (Singapore - Row 23)

sections.title: Outsourcing risks and regulatory expectations for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement robust risk management frameworks for outsourcing arrangements to mitigate reputation, compliance, operational, security, and country risks. The institution remains fully responsible for legal and regulatory compliance regardless of outsourcing. Institutions must self-assess existing outsourcing arrangements against MAS guidelines and ensure they cover all business activities, including those acquired through mergers or acquisitions.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing arrangements require risk management frameworks to address country and concentration risks when service providers are overseas. This section mandates that institutions maintain compliance obligations even when outsourcing services.

requirements.text: Institutions must adopt a sound and responsive risk management framework for outsourcing arrangements
Institutions must conduct self-assessment of all existing outsourcing arrangements against MAS guidelines

sections.title: Board and senior management oversight of outsourcing risk governance
sections.summary: The board and senior management must maintain oversight of outsourcing risks, including establishing risk appetite, approval authorities, and governance structures. They must ensure comprehensive risk assessments, effective risk mitigation, and regular reviews of outsourcing arrangements. The board remains ultimately responsible for the service provider's performance even if delegated to committees.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates robust governance frameworks for outsourcing risk management, requiring institutions to assess and mitigate risks before, during, and after outsourcing arrangements to prevent operational disruption and regulatory non-compliance.

requirements.text: Board must approve framework for evaluating risks and materiality of outsourcing arrangements
Board must set risk appetite for outsourcing arrangements
Board must establish approval authorities for outsourcing arrangements
Board must assess management competencies for outsourcing risk management
Board must ensure senior management establishes governance structures for risk management
Board must review outsourcing strategies for continued relevance and safety
Senior management must evaluate materiality and risks of outsourcing arrangements
Senior management must develop sound outsourcing policies and procedures
Senior management must review effectiveness of policies and procedures
Senior management must monitor risks from material outsourcing arrangements
Senior management must ensure contingency plans are in place and tested
Senior management must ensure independent review and audit of outsourcing compliance
Senior management must take timely remedial actions for audit findings
Senior management must communicate outsourcing risks to the board

sections.source_quote: The board and senior management of an institution play pivotal roles in ensuring a sound risk management culture and environment. While an institution may delegate day-to-day operational duties to the service provider, the responsibilities for maintaining effective oversight and governance of outsourcing arrangements, managing outsourcing risks, and implementing an adequate outsourcing risk management framework, in accordance with these Guidelines, continue to rest with the institution, its board and senior management.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Business Continuity for Outsourced Services
sections.summary: Financial institutions must proactively verify service providers' business continuity plans, requiring regular testing of recovery objectives and timely notification of test findings or significant changes. Institutions must ensure procedures exist to address adverse conditions or termination of outsourcing, including prompt removal of data from service providers. This applies to all outsourcing arrangements where service continuity is critical.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to validate service providers' business continuity capabilities and ensure data portability during outsourcing termination, critical for cross-border data transfers involving critical infrastructure.

requirements.text: Proactively seek assurance on the state of BCP preparedness of the service provider
Ensure service provider regularly tests BCP to ascertain recovery objectives
Require service provider to notify of test findings affecting performance
Require service provider to notify of substantial BCP changes
Require service provider to notify of adverse developments impacting service
Ensure plans exist to address adverse conditions or termination of outsourcing
Ensure prompt removal of documents, records and information from service provider

sections.source_quote: Proactively seek assurance on the state of BCP preparedness of the service provider. It should ensure the service provider regularly tests its BCP to ascertain that the recovery objectives can be met.

sections.heading: (continued)
sections.title: Material outsourcing arrangements for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must identify and assess material outsourcing arrangements based on factors including business impact, financial implications, reputation risks, customer impact, counterparty effects, cost proportions, and service provider failure consequences. Materiality is determined by the institution's ability to maintain internal controls and meet regulatory requirements if the service provider encounters operational issues. Institutions must also conduct periodic reviews to detect new outsourcing risks as services or volumes change.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires financial institutions to evaluate materiality of outsourcing arrangements to ensure adequate risk management for data transfers and processing across borders, particularly when outsourcing involves critical functions like risk management or internal controls.

requirements.text: An institution should assess materiality in outsourcing arrangements considering business activity importance, financial impact, reputation risks, customer impact, counterparty effects, cost proportions, and failure consequences

Outsourcing all or substantially all risk management or internal control functions, including compliance, internal audit, financial accounting and actuarial (other than certification activities), is material
Institutions should undertake periodic reviews to identify new outsourcing risks from incremental services, increased volumes, or service provider subcontracting
An institution should consider materiality at both institutional and group levels (branches and corporations under control)

sections.source_quote: An institution should assess the materiality in an outsourcing arrangement. In assessing materiality, MAS recognises that qualitative judgment is involved and the circumstances faced by individual institutions may vary.
